title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

examples.explanation: 6 units of water are trapped between the bars.
Water trapped: 2+0+3+1+0 units at various positions.
3 at index 1, 1 at index 2, 3 at index 3 = 7 total.

intuition: Think column by column. At each index i, how much water sits on top of it? Water is bounded by the tallest bar to the LEFT and the tallest bar to the RIGHT. The water level at i is min(max_left, max_right) — it fills up to the shorter of the two ceilings (water spills over the shorter one). Water trapped at i = water_level - height[i]. If water_level <= height[i], this is 0 or negative — meaning no water sits there (the bar itself is above the water level). Sum across all indices. The precomputation approach builds two arrays: max_left[i] = max height from index 0 to i, max_right[i] = max height from index i to n-1. Then a single pass computes the answer.
key_formula: water[i] = max(0, min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i]). The max(0, ...) handles cases where height[i] >= water_level — no water trapped, just 0. In practice, since max_left[i] >= height[i] and max_right[i] >= height[i] (they include height[i] itself), min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) >= height[i] always holds — so the subtraction is always non-negative. No explicit max(0,...) needed with this definition.
why_min_not_max: Water fills to the level of the SHORTER ceiling. If the left wall is height 5 and the right wall is height 3, water fills to level 3 — the left wall cannot hold water above the shorter right wall; it spills over. min captures this: water level = min(left ceiling, right ceiling). This is the same 'shorter wall caps the level' insight from Container With Most Water (LC 11).

why_include_self_in_max.max_left_definition: max_left[i] = max(height[0], height[1], ..., height[i]). It INCLUDES height[i] itself. This ensures max_left[i] >= height[i], so min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i] >= 0. No bar traps negative water.
why_include_self_in_max.first_element_fix: max_left[0] = height[0] is set explicitly BEFORE the forward loop (which starts at index 1). Without this: max_left[0] stays 0. Then for index 0: min(0, max_right[0]) - height[0] could be negative — wrong. Same fix for max_right at the last index.
why_include_self_in_max.boundary_semantics: Index 0 has no bars to its left — its left ceiling is its own height. Index n-1 has no bars to its right — its right ceiling is its own height. Both boundary bars trap 0 water (their water level equals their own height).

three_pass_structure.pass_1: Forward pass (left to right): build max_left[]. max_left[i] = max(height[i], max_left[i-1]). Each element is the running maximum seen so far from the left.
three_pass_structure.pass_2: Backward pass (right to left): build max_right[]. max_right[j] = max(height[j], max_right[j+1]). Each element is the running maximum seen so far from the right.
three_pass_structure.pass_3: Forward pass: for each i, water += min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i].

approach.name: Precompute max_left and max_right arrays (three-pass DP)
steps: Forward pass: for i from 1 to n-1: max_left[i] = max(height[i], max_left[i-1]).
Backward pass: for j from n-2 to 0: max_right[j] = max(height[j], max_right[j+1]).
Sum pass: res = 0. For each i: res += min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i].

code.solution:
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def trap(self, height: List[int]) -> int:

        # max_left[i] = tallest bar from index 0 to i (left ceiling)
        # start from 1 since no bars to the left of index 0
        max_left = [0] * len(height)

        # fix: without setting the first height you won't get correct trapped water
        max_left[0] = height[0]

        for i in range(1, len(height)):
            max_left[i] = max(height[i], max_left[i-1])

        # max_right[i] = tallest bar from index i to end (right ceiling)
        # start from second last since no bars to the right of last index
        max_right = [0] * len(height)

        # fix: same here for right as well
        max_right[len(height)-1] = height[len(height)-1]

        for j in range(len(height) - 2, -1, -1):
            max_right[j] = max(height[j], max_right[j+1])

        # water at i = min(left ceiling, right ceiling) - bar height
        # min because water spills over the shorter wall
        res = 0
        for i in range(len(height)):
            res += min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i]

        return res

        # Time Complexity:  O(n) — three linear passes
        # Space Complexity: O(n) — two extra arrays of size n

complexity.time: O(n) — three separate O(n) passes. Total: O(3n) = O(n).
complexity.space: O(n) — max_left array of size n and max_right array of size n. Reducible to O(1) with the two-pointer approach.

No: max_right[11]=2. max_right[10]=max(1,2)=2. max_right[9]=max(0,2)=2. max_right[8]=max(1,2)=2. max_right[7]=max(3,2)=3. So for i=8: min(max_left[8],max_right[8])-height[8]=min(3,2)-1=2-1=1. two_pointer_O1_space.name: Two Pointers (O(1) space)
two_pointer_O1_space.description: Instead of precomputing full max_left and max_right arrays, maintain running left_max and right_max variables. Process from both ends simultaneously. At each step, the pointer with the smaller max determines the water at its position — because the other side is guaranteed to be at least as tall.
two_pointer_O1_space.why_works: When height[left] <= height[right]: we know right_max >= height[right] >= height[left]. So the water at left is determined by left_max (the left ceiling is the bottleneck). Water = left_max - height[left]. We don't need to know the exact right ceiling — we just need to know it's at least as tall as left_max, which height[right] >= height[left] implies.

monotonic_stack.name: Monotonic Stack
monotonic_stack.description: Process bars left to right. Maintain a stack of indices with decreasing heights. When a taller bar is found, it can trap water against the bars in the stack. Pop the stack, compute the width and bounded height between the popped bar and the current bar, add to result.
monotonic_stack.when_to_use: When the interviewer asks for an alternative approach. The stack approach computes water horizontally (row by row) rather than vertically (column by column).

rows: Column by column: water = min(left_max, right_max) - height
Same column logic but compute maxes on the fly
Horizontal slabs between consecutive tall bars

connection_to_lc11: Container With Most Water (LC 11) finds the BEST pair of walls. Trapping Rain Water (LC 42) finds water trapped between ALL walls. Both use min(left, right) as the water level. LC 11 is the simpler warm-up. LC 42 applies the same insight at every column simultaneously.
python_gotchas: max_left[0] = height[0] must be set BEFORE the loop starting at range(1, n). Without it, max_left[0] stays 0. Water at index 0 = min(0, max_right[0]) - height[0] which can be negative if height[0] > 0.
max_right[len(height)-1] = height[len(height)-1] must be set BEFORE the backward loop starting at range(len(height)-2, -1, -1). Same reason.

range(len(height) - 2, -1, -1): start at n-2 (second to last), stop before -1 (exclusive, so index 0 IS included), step -1 (backwards). A common mistake: range(n-2, 0, -1) stops before 0 — missing index 0.
min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i] is always >= 0 with this definition (since max_left and max_right include height[i] itself). No need for max(0, ...) guard.
The three loops can be combined — but splitting them makes the logic clearer. The two-pointer approach does it in one loop at the cost of more complex logic.
res += ... not res = ... inside the final loop. Accumulate, don't overwrite.

related_problems.title: Container With Most Water (LC 11)
related_problems.relation: Simpler version — find the single best pair of walls. Same min(left, right) water level insight. LC 11 is the recommended prerequisite.

related_problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LC 84)
related_problems.relation: Companion problem — instead of water trapped between bars, find the largest rectangle that fits within bars. Uses monotonic stack. Many solutions solve both together.

related_problems.title: Product of Array Except Self (LC 238)
related_problems.relation: Same structural pattern — precompute a left-pass array and a right-pass array, then combine. Left prefix products + right prefix products. Directly analogous to left max + right max here.

related_problems.title: Maximum Width Ramp (LC 962)
related_problems.relation: Also uses precomputed left and right structures to avoid O(n^2) pair checking.

mcqs.question: What does max_left[i] represent, and why does it include height[i] itself?
options: max_left[i] = maximum height strictly to the LEFT of index i (excludes height[i])
max_left[i] = maximum height from index 0 to i INCLUSIVE. It includes height[i] so that min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i] >= 0 always — no bar can trap negative water.
max_left[i] = height[i] only, ignoring previous bars
max_left[i] = average height of all bars to the left
mcqs.explanation: max_left[i] = max(height[0], height[1], ..., height[i]). Including height[i] guarantees max_left[i] >= height[i]. Similarly max_right[i] >= height[i]. Therefore min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) >= height[i], ensuring water[i] = min(...) - height[i] >= 0. If we excluded height[i] (strict left max), max_left[i] could be less than height[i] for boundary bars, giving negative water — physically wrong.

mcqs.question: Why must max_left[0] = height[0] be set before the forward loop?
options: The loop starts at index 1, so index 0 is never touched by the loop

Without it, max_left[0] stays 0 (default initialization). If height[0] > 0, then water[0] = min(0, max_right[0]) - height[0] = -height[0] < 0. Negative water is wrong. Setting max_left[0] = height[0] correctly states: the tallest bar from 0 to 0 is height[0].
It handles the edge case where height has only one element
It prevents the loop from accessing index -1
mcqs.explanation: The forward loop runs range(1, n) — it starts at index 1, never updating index 0. Without the explicit initialization, max_left[0]=0. At the final pass: water[0]=min(0, max_right[0])-height[0]. Since max_right[0] is usually tall and min(0, anything)=0: water[0]= 0-height[0] which is negative when height[0]>0. This overcounts water. The fix: max_left[0]=height[0] gives water[0]=min(height[0], max_right[0])-height[0]=0 (a bar traps no water above itself). Correct.

mcqs.question: Why is water at each index computed as min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i] and not max(...) - height[i]?
options: min is used for efficiency — max would be O(n^2)

Water fills to the level of the SHORTER ceiling. If the left wall is taller than the right, water spills over the right wall at the right ceiling's level. min captures: water rises to the minimum of the two ceilings. Using max would claim water rises above the shorter wall — physically impossible.
max would give a negative result for tall bars
The two are equivalent when the array is sorted
mcqs.explanation: At index i, the left 'wall' is max_left[i] and the right 'wall' is max_right[i]. Water fills like a bucket — it rises until it spills over the shorter side. If max_left=5 and max_right=3: water fills to level 3 (spills right). If we used max(5,3)=5: we'd claim water rises to 5 — it would spill over the right wall at 3, so only 3 units high. min(5,3)=3 is correct. Identical insight to LC 11 Container With Most Water.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity and how many passes does the algorithm make?
options: O(n^2) — nested loops for left and right max at each index
O(n) — exactly three linear passes: one forward (max_left), one backward (max_right), one forward (sum water). Total O(3n) = O(n).
O(n log n) — the max operations require sorting
O(n) but only for sorted arrays
mcqs.explanation: Pass 1: forward O(n) to build max_left. Pass 2: backward O(n) to build max_right. Pass 3: forward O(n) to compute and sum water. Each pass does O(1) work per element. Total: O(3n) = O(n). No sorting, no nested loops.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity and how can it be reduced to O(1)?
options: O(n) — max_left and max_right arrays each of size n. Reduce to O(1) using the two-pointer approach: maintain left_max and right_max as running scalars, process from both ends simultaneously.
O(1) — the three loops use constant space
O(n^2) — two arrays of size n each
O(log n) — recursion depth of the backward pass
mcqs.explanation: max_left: O(n). max_right: O(n). Total auxiliary space: O(n). The two-pointer approach replaces both arrays with two integer variables (left_max, right_max) and two pointers, using O(1) extra space while maintaining O(n) time. The tradeoff: O(1) space requires more complex single-pass logic vs the cleaner three-pass approach.

mcqs.question: height = [4,2,0,3,2,5]. What is max_left[2] and max_right[2]?
options: max_left[2]=0, max_right[2]=5
max_left[2]=4 (max of heights 0-2: max(4,2,0)=4), max_right[2]=5 (max of heights 2-5: max(0,3,2,5)=5)
max_left[2]=2, max_right[2]=3
max_left[2]=4, max_right[2]=3
mcqs.explanation: max_left[2] = max(height[0..2]) = max(4,2,0) = 4. max_right[2] = max(height[2..5]) = max(0,3,2,5) = 5. Water at index 2: min(4,5) - 0 = 4 - 0 = 4 units. This is the deepest point in the example — 4 units of water sit on top of the empty bar at index 2.

mcqs.question: For the backward loop 'range(len(height)-2, -1, -1)', why is the stop value -1?
options: To make the range exclusive of index 0 so we don't overwrite max_right[0]

range(start, stop, step) excludes the stop value. To include index 0: stop must be -1 (since -1 is excluded, 0 is the last index processed). Using range(n-2, 0, -1) would stop before 0 — missing index 0. Same gotcha as all backward-range loops in Python.
The -1 stop handles negative indices in the array
It prevents accessing max_right[-1] which would wrap around
mcqs.explanation: range(n-2, -1, -1) generates: n-2, n-3, ..., 1, 0. Stops before -1, so 0 IS included. range(n-2, 0, -1) generates: n-2, n-3, ..., 1. Stops before 0, so 0 is NOT included — max_right[0] would never be computed. Since max_right[n-1] is set before the loop, the loop needs to cover n-2 down to 0 inclusive.

mcqs.question: height = [3,0,2,0,4]. Compute water at each index.
options: Total = 5
Total = 4
mcqs.explanation: max_left: [3,3,3,3,4]. max_right: [4,4,4,4,4]. Water[1]=min(3,4)-0=3. Water[2]=min(3,4)-2=1. Water[3]=min(3,4)-0=3. Total=0+3+1+3+0=7. Correct — matches the example.

mcqs.question: Why does the code use 'max_left[i] = max(height[i], max_left[i-1])' not 'max_left[i] = max(max_left[i-1], max_left[i-1])'?
options: It's a typo — both forms are equivalent

max_left[i] = max(height[i], max_left[i-1]) correctly takes the max of the CURRENT bar (height[i]) and the running max (max_left[i-1]). Using max_left[i-1] twice would never incorporate height[i] — the running max would never update. max_left would just copy max_left[i-1] forever.
height[i] is needed for the initialization of the first element
max_left[i-1] alone is the correct value for max_left[i]
mcqs.explanation: max_left[i] should be the tallest bar seen from 0 to i. To compute this from max_left[i-1] (tallest from 0 to i-1), we compare with height[i] (the current bar) and take the max. max(height[i], max_left[i-1]) = max of 'new bar' and 'previous running max'. Using max(max_left[i-1], max_left[i-1]) = max_left[i-1] — the current bar is never considered. max_left would freeze at height[0] forever.

mcqs.question: How does the two-pointer O(1) approach know which side to process?
options: It always processes left first, then right

When height[left] <= height[right]: the right side is at least as tall. The left side is the bottleneck. Water at left = left_max - height[left] (left ceiling caps the level — the right side is guaranteed >= left_max). Process left and advance left. Symmetrically for the right side.
The side with the smaller running max is always processed
Both sides are processed simultaneously in parallel
mcqs.explanation: The key insight: if height[left] <= height[right], then we KNOW right_max >= height[right] >= height[left] >= left_max (eventually). The right side is guaranteed to be at least as tall as the left. Water at left is fully determined by left_max. We don't need to see the rest of the right side. Process left, update left_max, advance left. This is why the two-pointer approach is correct: whichever side is shorter, its max is the bottleneck.

mcqs.question: height = [1,0,1]. What are max_left, max_right, and total water?
options: max_left=[1,1,1], max_right=[1,1,1]. Water: 0+1+0=1.
max_left=[1,0,1], max_right=[1,0,1]. Water: 0+0+0=0.
max_left=[0,1,1], max_right=[1,1,0]. Water=2.
Total=0 — bars of equal height trap no water
mcqs.explanation: max_left[0]=1. max_left[1]=max(0,1)=1. max_left[2]=max(1,1)=1. max_right[2]=1. max_right[1]=max(0,1)=1. max_right[0]=max(1,1)=1. Water[0]:min(1,1)-1=0. Water[1]:min(1,1)-0=1. Water[2]:min(1,1)-1=0. Total=1.

mcqs.question: This problem shares the same structural pattern as Product of Array Except Self (LC 238). What is the analogy?
options: Both use multiplication — no structural similarity

Both precompute a LEFT array (running accumulation left-to-right) and a RIGHT array (running accumulation right-to-left), then combine. LC 238: left_product[i] and right_product[i], combined by multiplication. LC 42: max_left[i] and max_right[i], combined by min then subtraction. The three-pass structure is identical.
Both problems require sorting before the DP passes
Both are reducible to sliding window problems
mcqs.explanation: LC 238 Product Except Self: pass 1 builds left_products (running product L to R), pass 2 builds right_products (running product R to L), pass 3 multiplies them. LC 42 Trapping Rain Water: pass 1 builds max_left (running max L to R), pass 2 builds max_right (running max R to L), pass 3 takes min and subtracts height. Structurally identical: two directional passes + combination. The accumulated value is product vs max; the combination is multiply vs min-minus.

mcqs.question: Can there be negative water at any index with this algorithm?
options: Yes — when height[i] exceeds both max_left and max_right

No — by definition max_left[i] >= height[i] (includes height[i]) and max_right[i] >= height[i] (includes height[i]). Therefore min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) >= height[i]. The subtraction is always >= 0.
Yes — at boundary indices 0 and n-1
It depends on whether the heights are sorted
mcqs.explanation: max_left[i] = max(height[0..i]) >= height[i] (height[i] is one of the values in the max). max_right[i] = max(height[i..n-1]) >= height[i]. So min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) >= height[i]. Water[i] = min(...) - height[i] >= 0. No index can have negative water. Boundary indices: water[0] = min(height[0], max_right[0]) - height[0] = height[0] - height[0] = 0 (since max_left[0]=height[0]).

mcqs.question: height = [0,0,0,0]. What is the total trapped water?
options: 0 — all bars have height 0, no water can be trapped
4 — four positions with water
1 — one unit at the center
An error — cannot trap water with zero-height bars
mcqs.explanation: max_left=[0,0,0,0], max_right=[0,0,0,0]. At every index: min(0,0)-0=0. Total=0. Correct — all-zero-height arrays trap no water. The surface is flat at level 0 everywhere.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between max_left[n-1] and the total maximum height in the array?
options: max_left[n-1] equals the average height

max_left[n-1] = max(height[0..n-1]) = maximum height in the entire array. After the full forward pass, the last element of max_left holds the global maximum. Similarly max_right[0] = global maximum after the backward pass.
max_left[n-1] = height[n-1] — only the last bar
max_left[n-1] = n * max height
mcqs.explanation: The forward pass computes a running maximum. At each step, the running max either stays the same or increases. By the final index n-1, the running max has seen all n elements — it equals the global maximum. max_left[n-1] = max(all heights). Similarly, max_right[0] = max(all heights) after the backward pass. Both boundary maximums equal the global maximum.

mcqs.question: height = [5,4,3,2,1] (strictly decreasing). How much water is trapped?
options: 10 — water accumulates on the declining slope

0 — there is no right wall taller than any left position. max_right[i] = height[i] for all i (each bar is the max from itself to the end). Water[i]=min(max_left[i], height[i])-height[i]=min(max_left[i],height[i])-height[i]. Since max_left[i]>=height[i] and max_right[i]=height[i]: min=height[i]. Water[i]=height[i]-height[i]=0.
5 — the tallest bar traps water
15 — sum of all heights
mcqs.explanation: Decreasing array: max_right[i] = height[i] (each bar is tallest from itself to the end — all bars to the right are shorter). Water[i] = min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) - height[i] = min(max_left[i], height[i]) - height[i]. Since max_left[i] >= height[i]: min = height[i]. Water[i] = 0. A strictly decreasing array traps no water — there is always a downhill slope, nothing holds water back from the right.

mcqs.question: The monotonic stack approach computes water differently. How does it conceptually differ from the column-by-column approach?
options: The stack approach is O(n^2) — slower than the DP approach

Column-by-column (your solution): at each index, compute water trapped vertically (column of water above the bar). Stack approach: compute water trapped horizontally (row by row between bars). The stack finds 'bathtubs' — pairs of bounding bars with a valley between them — and fills them layer by layer. Both give the same total but the mental model differs.
The stack approach cannot handle duplicate heights
The stack processes elements right to left only
mcqs.explanation: Column approach: at index i, water = min(left_max, right_max) - height[i]. Think of each position as a vertical column filled with water. Stack approach: when a bar taller than the stack's top is found, water fills the horizontal gap between the stack's new top and the current bar. Think of filling horizontal slabs. Both compute the same total water — they decompose the 2D water region differently (vertical slices vs horizontal slices).

mcqs.question: height = [1,2,3,2,1] (symmetric pyramid). How much water is trapped?
options: 0 — symmetric arrays trap no water
5 — water fills the outer portions
0 — it IS a pyramid, water runs off the sides immediately
mcqs.explanation: max_left=[1,2,3,3,3]. max_right=[3,3,3,2,1]. At every index: min(max_left[i], max_right[i]) = height[i] or less. Specifically: i=0: min(1,3)-1=0. i=1: min(2,3)-2=0. i=2: min(3,3)-3=0. i=3: min(3,2)-2=0. i=4: min(3,1)-1=0. Total=0. A pyramid shape traps no water — it slopes up then down, with no 'valleys' to hold water.

mcqs.question: If height has only one element, what does the algorithm return?
options: An IndexError — the loops access out-of-bounds indices
0 — max_left[0]=height[0], max_right[0]=height[0]. The sum loop: water[0]=min(height[0],height[0])-height[0]=0. Return 0. A single bar traps no water.
height[0] — the bar's height is the water
The constraints say n>=1 so single element is valid — returns 0
mcqs.explanation: n=1. max_left=[height[0]]. Backward loop: range(n-2=-1, -1, -1) is empty — no iterations. max_right=[height[0]]. Sum loop: water[0]=min(height[0],height[0])-height[0]=0. Return 0. A single bar with nothing on either side traps no water. The constraints say n>=1, so this edge case must be handled. It is.

mcqs.question: Why is the third loop a separate pass instead of being merged with the second (backward) pass?
options: Merging would change the time complexity

The third loop needs BOTH max_left[i] and max_right[i]. max_left is built left-to-right. max_right is built right-to-left. At any point during the backward pass, max_left[i] is already complete but max_right[i] is being built now — however, the previous max_right values (for i+1 to n-1) are complete. You COULD merge passes 2 and 3 by iterating backward: compute max_right[i] then use max_left[i] (already known) to add water. The three-pass version is cleaner and clearer.
Merging would cause incorrect water values at boundary indices
Python cannot merge backward and forward loops
mcqs.explanation: Technically, you can merge the backward pass and water summation: iterate right-to-left, computing max_right[i] on the fly and adding water. At each i, max_left[i] is already in the max_left array (computed in pass 1), and you can use it immediately. This reduces to two passes. The three-pass version splits concerns cleanly: build left array, build right array, combine. Both are O(n). The two-pointer approach achieves O(1) space by doing everything in one pass.

mcqs.question: height = [2,0,2]. What is the water trapped?
options: 0 — the two outer bars are the same height
2 — the bar at index 0 is height 2, so 2 units fill over index 1
1 — only 1 unit fits in the middle
2 — water fills the middle to level min(2,2)=2. Water[1]=min(2,2)-0=2.
mcqs.explanation: max_left=[2,2,2]. max_right=[2,2,2]. Water[0]=min(2,2)-2=0. Water[1]=min(2,2)-0=2. Water[2]=min(2,2)-2=0. Total=2. The valley at index 1 (height 0) is surrounded by walls of height 2 on both sides. Water fills to level 2. 2-0=2 units trapped.

mcqs.question: In the two-pointer approach, what does 'left_max' track — and how is it different from max_left in the three-pass approach?
options: They are identical — both track the global running maximum

Both track the running maximum from the left. The difference: in the three-pass approach, max_left is precomputed for ALL indices before being used. In the two-pointer approach, left_max is computed on the fly as left pointer advances. At any moment, left_max = max(height[0..left]) — only accurate for the current left position, not all positions. Right_max similarly tracks right-to-left running max.
left_max in two-pointer tracks the minimum, not maximum
left_max is only used when height[left] > height[right]
mcqs.explanation: Three-pass: precompute max_left[0..n-1] fully, then use all values in pass 3. Two-pointer: maintain left_max as the running maximum only for the current left pointer position. The two-pointer approach works because it processes each element exactly once — when the element is processed, left_max is accurate for that element. The tradeoff: three-pass precomputes everything (uses O(n) space), two-pointer computes on demand (uses O(1) space).

mcqs.question: The code initializes 'max_left = [0] * len(height)'. Is there a simpler one-liner alternative for building max_left?
options: max_left = sorted(height)
max_left = list(itertools.accumulate(height, max)) — accumulate with max as the function builds the running maximum in one line. No explicit loop needed.
max_left = [max(height)] * len(height)
max_left = height.copy()
mcqs.explanation: itertools.accumulate(height, max) generates: height[0], max(height[0],height[1]), max(max(height[0],height[1]),height[2]), ... — exactly the running maximum. list(itertools.accumulate(height, max)) builds max_left in one line, no explicit loop, no initialization needed. For max_right: list(reversed(list(itertools.accumulate(reversed(height), max)))). Both are O(n). The explicit loop version is clearer for learning.

mcqs.question: height = [0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,1,0,1,2]. At index 5 (height=0), how much water is trapped?
options: 0 — height is 0 so no bar exists
2 — min(max_left[5], max_right[5]) - height[5] = min(2,3) - 0 = 2.
3 — the right wall is height 3
1 — only 1 unit fits at this position
mcqs.explanation: max_left[5] = max(0,1,0,2,1,0) = 2. max_right[5] = max(0,1,3,1,0,1,2) = 3. Water[5] = min(2,3) - 0 = 2. The left ceiling is 2 (the bar at index 3), the right ceiling is 3 (the bar at index 7). Water fills to level 2 (shorter ceiling). The bar at index 5 has height 0, so 2 units of water sit on top.

mcqs.question: An interviewer asks: 'Can you reduce the space from O(n) to O(1) without changing the time complexity?' What do you answer?
options: No — you need to store max_left and max_right for all positions simultaneously

Yes — use the two-pointer approach. Maintain left_max and right_max as scalars. Use two pointers (left=0, right=n-1) converging inward. At each step, if height[left] <= height[right]: the right side is tall enough — water at left = left_max - height[left]; update left_max; advance left. Else: water at right = right_max - height[right]; update right_max; advance right. O(n) time, O(1) space.
Yes — sort the array first so only one pass is needed
Yes — use bit manipulation to compress the max arrays
mcqs.explanation: The two-pointer approach is the standard O(1) space answer. Key insight: when height[left] <= height[right], the right side's max is irrelevant — we know it's at least height[right] >= height[left] >= left_max. The left side is the bottleneck. We can compute water at left without knowing the full right side. This observation allows processing one element per step with just two scalar max-trackers instead of two full arrays.
